ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to beef shank; A0CKL (cooked beef meat) used as closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised beef shank in a Chinese restaurant context is approximately 150 g of sliced, slow-cooked shank meat, often served as a side dish or as part of a noodle meal.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, shank crosscuts, separable lean and fat, cooked, simmered — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked beef shank (simmered, lean and fat) used as the primary nutrient basis. Values represent cooked weight per 100 g. Chinese braised preparation may add minor sodium from soy sauce and aromatics; sodium value adjusted upward to reflect typical Chinese braised preparation based on USDA Branded Food comparators. All other macros retained from USDA SR Legacy cooked shank values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure cooked beef contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g). Meat and fish are universally considered effectively zero-GI. Per Veritas v2 rules for zero-GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is low. Glycemic load per 150 g serving is 0.0.

hypertension.guidance: Chinese braised beef shank can contain 300–450 mg sodium per 100 g due to soy sauce and seasoning in the braising liquid; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request reduced-sodium preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef shank contains essentially zero digestible carbohydrate and has an effectively zero glycemic index (set to 1 per convention), making it a suitable high-protein, low-GI protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; portion control remains important due to saturated fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef shank provides approximately 3.3 g saturated fat and 88 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to 100–120 g per serving and favour lean trimmed portions to reduce saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef shank (牛腱, niú jiàn) is a cut of beef taken from the lower leg of the animal, characterised by dense muscle fibres, a central marrow bone (in cross-cut form), and a high proportion of connective tissue rich in collagen. In Chinese cuisine, beef shank is most commonly prepared by slow braising or simmering in a spiced soy-based master stock containing aromatics such as star anise, ginger, garlic, Shaoxing rice wine, and dried chillies. The prolonged cooking process — typically two to three hours — converts the collagen into gelatin, yielding meat that is tender, moist, and slightly gelatinous in texture with a deeply savoury, umami-rich flavour. The dish is a staple accompaniment in Lanzhou-style beef noodle restaurants and is commonly served sliced thinly over noodle soups or as a cold appetiser. Nutritionally, braised beef shank is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 28.6 g protein and 9.0 g fat per 100 g cooked weight. It is a meaningful source of iron (2.8 mg), zinc (6.1 mg), vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg), and selenium (28 mcg). The sodium content is elevated due to the soy-based braising liquid, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is effectively zero (set to 1 per convention) as the food contains no digestible carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-GI dietary patterns and Type 2 Diabetes management. The saturated fat content warrants moderation for cardiovascular health.

Is beef shank good for diabetics?

meal_context: protein component of a Chinese meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy for cooked beef shank (simmered), which is a close match to the Chinese braised preparation. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical Chinese braised preparation. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for braised beef shank was identified. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — cooked beef shank contains no digestible carbohydrate. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — no sugars present in plain cooked beef shank. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — meat contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — cooked beef contains no vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1.0: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this cut. vitamin_d_mcg is -1.0: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this cut. vitamin_e_mg is -1.0: not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this cut. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as beef shank contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per Veritas v2 convention for zero-GI foods. Sodium value of 380 mg per 100 g reflects typical Chinese braised preparation with soy sauce; raw USDA value for plain simmered shank is approximately 65 mg per 100 g. field survey source context: this record originates from Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles, Singapore. Halal status marked true as Lanzhou beef noodle restaurants in Singapore typically serve halal-certified beef; this should be verified at the restaurant level.
